g.data-narration: Software Project Management covers all activities from project initiation through closure to deliver software successfully.
g.text: Activities Covered by Software Project Management

g.data-narration: SPM applies knowledge and skills to plan, execute, monitor, and control software projects to meet stakeholder requirements.
g.text: The application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to plan, execute,
monitor, and control software development projects to meet stakeholder requirements.
SPM addresses unique challenges: time, cost, quality, and changing requirements

g.data-narration: Research shows significant percentage of software projects fail due to inadequate management practices, making SPM crucial.
g.text: • Ensures projects delivered on time, within budget, with required quality
• Research shows significant % of software projects fail due to poor management
Essential for academic success and professional practice in software industry

g.data-narration: Project initiation identifies the need for a new system and defines scope. Planning creates detailed project plans with WBS and schedules.
Project estimation predicts effort, duration, and cost using techniques like Function Points, COCOMO, and story points.
g.text: Predicting effort (person-hours), duration (calendar time), and cost (financial resources)

g.data-narration: Resource management identifies, acquires, and deploys human, physical, and intangible resources needed for project success.
Risk management proactively identifies, analyzes, and responds to potential risks through mitigation, transfer, avoidance, or acceptance.
g.text: Proactive process: Identify → Analyze → Respond → Monitor

g.data-narration: Project scheduling uses Gantt charts, CPM, and PERT. Tracking monitors progress against plans using Earned Value Management.
Quality management ensures software meets standards through planning, assurance (process), and control (product inspection).
Configuration management tracks and controls changes to software artifacts, preventing chaos in multi-developer projects.
Communication management ensures efficient information exchange among stakeholders through planned channels and regular meetings.
Project closure formally completes the project through delivery, post-project reviews, and documenting lessons learned.
Function Point Analysis estimates effort: 50 FP at 20 FP/person-month yields 2.5 person-months, costing approximately Rs 2,10,000.
g.text: 50 Function Points estimated with productivity rate of 20 FP/person-month

g.data-narration: Risk response strategies include cross-training, documentation, external consultants, and contingency plans for schedule adjustments.
g.text: Risk: Key developer unavailability (Probability: 40%, Impact: High - 2 week delay)
MITIGATE: Cross-train team, maintain documentation, establish external consultant relationships
CONTINGENCY: Reallocate work, adjust schedule if risk materializes

g.data-narration: EVM shows project behind schedule (SPI=0.8) and over budget (CPI=0.89). EAC predicts Rs 12,36,000 needed to complete.
g.text: Example 3: Earned Value Analysis (Rs 10L Project, Month 5)

g.data-narration: Key formulas: Function Points, Effort, SPI, CPI, EAC, and ETC - essential for SPM exam numerical problems.
g.text: Cost = Effort x Rate + Infrastructure + Contingency
EAC = BAC / CPI, ETC = EAC - AC

g.data-narration: Remember: QA is process-oriented (prevention), QC is product-oriented (inspection). Practice EVM numerical problems for exams.
g.text: • Treating risk management as one-time activity
• Skipping project closure and lessons learned
• SPI/CPI less than 1 = behind schedule/over budget

g.data-narration: SPM activities span the entire project lifecycle, integrating estimation, risk, quality, configuration, communication, and closure.
g.text: 9 Key SPM Activities: Initiation Planning Estimation Resource Risk
All activities documented and integrated for successful project delivery
